Prompt tuning is a key component of CLIP's PEFT techniques. However, existing methods primarily rely on the LLM-prior alignment paradigm, which is limited by the quality of LLM priors and the semantic distribution gap, significantly affecting CLIP's performance on unseen categories. To address this issue, we propose Omni-Tuning, a novel shared space guided paradigm. Unlike traditional LLM-prior alignment, Omni-Tuning learns shared semantic features between seen and unseen classes, fitting the distributions of these two subsets. Since this distribution simulates the shared features between seen and unseen categories, it serves as a robust guide to improve the model's generalization ability, even when prior knowledge is insufficient or unreliable. Specifically, Omni-Tuning divides the base classes into meta-base and meta-new subsets and uses Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to learn their shared semantic features, referred to as OmniPrompt. Additionally, we introduce the Geometry-Guided Prompt Adjustment (GPA) mechanism, which maps image and text features into OmniPrompt's shared distribution space. This helps the model extract features that align with the shared distribution, effectively addressing the semantic distribution gap while retaining original prior knowledge, thus improving the alignment of unseen category visual features. Experimental results demonstrate that Omni-Tuning significantly enhances generalization to unseen categories, overcoming the limitations of LLM-prior alignment and achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks.

item.title: From Recognition to Reasoning: Edge-Frequency Chain-of-Thought for Remote Sensing Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Yuzhen Zhao
Xun Li
Baoxi Yuan
Yang Zhao
Ruijuan Yao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: The main bottleneck of current object detectors does not lie in weak feature representation. It lies in the lack of structured reasoning. Most detectors follow a recognition-style pipeline. They map features directly to detection results. This design ignores structural relations inside visual information. As a result, model stability and robustness degrade in complex scenes. This paper proposes Edge-Frequency Chain-of-Thought (EF-CoT), a structured reasoning paradigm for object detection. EF-CoT reformulates detection as a step-wise reasoning process. It jointly models domain-specific tokens, spatial priors, and progressive reasoning. The model analyzes visual evidence before making predictions. Based on EF-CoT, this paper designs the Triple-branch Guided Frequency Alignment and Edge-aware Network (TGFAENet). TGFAENet builds edge-frequency-aware structured tokens. Token-level reasoning then models the interaction among global structure, local details, and boundary cues. The Gaussian Attention Spatial Pyramid Pooling Fusion (GASPPF) module is further introduced. GASPPF uses dual-branch spatial pyramid pooling and Gaussian attention to model continuous spatial priors. This module guides the detector toward potential target regions. By combining structured token modeling, spatial awareness, and chain-style reasoning, the proposed method builds a unified perception-analysis-decision detection framework. Experiments on complex remote sensing scenes show clear performance gains. The gains are more visible on small-object detection. These results support the shift from direct recognition to diagnostic reasoning in object detection. Published: 2026-07-20T20:19:54+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.787 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuzhen Zhao; Xun Li; Baoxi Yuan; Yang Zhao; Ruijuan Yao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3715352

Relevance: 0.787 (must_read)

The main bottleneck of current object detectors does not lie in weak feature representation. It lies in the lack of structured reasoning. Most detectors follow a recognition-style pipeline. They map features directly to detection results. This design ignores structural relations inside visual information. As a result, model stability and robustness degrade in complex scenes. This paper proposes Edge-Frequency Chain-of-Thought (EF-CoT), a structured reasoning paradigm for object detection. EF-CoT reformulates detection as a step-wise reasoning process. It jointly models domain-specific tokens, spatial priors, and progressive reasoning. The model analyzes visual evidence before making predictions. Based on EF-CoT, this paper designs the Triple-branch Guided Frequency Alignment and Edge-aware Network (TGFAENet). TGFAENet builds edge-frequency-aware structured tokens. Token-level reasoning then models the interaction among global structure, local details, and boundary cues. The Gaussian Attention Spatial Pyramid Pooling Fusion (GASPPF) module is further introduced. GASPPF uses dual-branch spatial pyramid pooling and Gaussian attention to model continuous spatial priors. This module guides the detector toward potential target regions. By combining structured token modeling, spatial awareness, and chain-style reasoning, the proposed method builds a unified perception-analysis-decision detection framework. Experiments on complex remote sensing scenes show clear performance gains. The gains are more visible on small-object detection. These results support the shift from direct recognition to diagnostic reasoning in object detection.

item.title: MultiLoReFT: Decoupling Shared and Modality-Specific Subspaces in Multimodal Learning via Low-Rank Representation Fine-Tuning
item.dc:creator: Sana Tonekaboni
Viktoria Schuster
Caroline Uhler
item.description: Real-world perception and decision making are inherently multimodal, integrating complementary signals across modalities. However, training multimodal models faces two main obstacles. First, collecting large-scale, well-aligned paired multimodal datasets is often impractical, making end-to-end multimodal training difficult. Second, existing multimodal representations frequently entangle information shared across modalities with modality-specific information, hindering interpretability and control. We introduce MultiLoReFT, an efficient and scalable low-rank representation fine-tuning framework for multimodal learning with pretrained unimodal models. MultiLoReFT extends low-rank adaptation to the multimodal setting and learns interpretable projection subspaces that decouple shared and modality-specific information. Across simulated and real-world benchmarks, it produces representations that support multimodal prediction while explicitly revealing how shared and modality-specific information is distributed across modalities. Published: 2026-07-18T11:53:22+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.785 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sana Tonekaboni; Viktoria Schuster; Caroline Uhler

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.785 (must_read)

Real-world perception and decision making are inherently multimodal, integrating complementary signals across modalities. However, training multimodal models faces two main obstacles. First, collecting large-scale, well-aligned paired multimodal datasets is often impractical, making end-to-end multimodal training difficult. Second, existing multimodal representations frequently entangle information shared across modalities with modality-specific information, hindering interpretability and control. We introduce MultiLoReFT, an efficient and scalable low-rank representation fine-tuning framework for multimodal learning with pretrained unimodal models. MultiLoReFT extends low-rank adaptation to the multimodal setting and learns interpretable projection subspaces that decouple shared and modality-specific information. Across simulated and real-world benchmarks, it produces representations that support multimodal prediction while explicitly revealing how shared and modality-specific information is distributed across modalities.

item.title: DeeperRadar: End-to-End MIMO Radar Design and Multi-Modal Fusion for Autonomous Vehicle Perception
item.dc:creator: Eli Goldenshluger
Barak Pinkovich
Chaim Baskin
item.description: DeeperRadar is a radar-centric, sensor-stack-conditioned framework that co-designs radar sensing and multi-modal 3D detection for autonomous mobility by learning a sparse acquisition pattern end-to-end with the fusion model. A learnable MIMO design module is trained end-to-end within a fusion network that operates directly on raw radar ADC data together with camera images and LiDAR point clouds. During training, the design module is supervised by the other sensors, enabling the system to learn both which receiver antennas to activate and the effective number of them. At deployment, the design module is removed and replaced by the learned sparse subsampling mask, leaving the downstream model architecture unchanged. Evaluated on the RADIal dataset, DeeperRadar discovers sparse, task-aware radar configurations that match or exceed full-array baselines while using fewer receivers, potentially reducing radar cost and integration complexity. These results show that learned optimal MIMO radar design depends on the fusion stack and the downstream perception task. Published: 2026-07-19T17:26:34+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.784 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Eli Goldenshluger; Barak Pinkovich; Chaim Baskin

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

DeeperRadar is a radar-centric, sensor-stack-conditioned framework that co-designs radar sensing and multi-modal 3D detection for autonomous mobility by learning a sparse acquisition pattern end-to-end with the fusion model. A learnable MIMO design module is trained end-to-end within a fusion network that operates directly on raw radar ADC data together with camera images and LiDAR point clouds. During training, the design module is supervised by the other sensors, enabling the system to learn both which receiver antennas to activate and the effective number of them. At deployment, the design module is removed and replaced by the learned sparse subsampling mask, leaving the downstream model architecture unchanged. Evaluated on the RADIal dataset, DeeperRadar discovers sparse, task-aware radar configurations that match or exceed full-array baselines while using fewer receivers, potentially reducing radar cost and integration complexity. These results show that learned optimal MIMO radar design depends on the fusion stack and the downstream perception task.

item.title: Rethinking Transformer for Multi-Object Tracking with Relative Position Perception
item.dc:creator: Ziyi Liu
Canbin Zhang
Xueli Li
Bin Fan
Yi Wu
Hongmin Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Transformer-based methods have achieved remarkable success in Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) by leveraging attention mechanisms to capture global dependencies. However, these approaches typically inherit design frameworks from object detection tasks, failing to address the unique challenges of MOT—particularly the critical role of relative positional information. This limitation hinders their performance in complex scenarios involving interactive and dynamic targets. To address this, we introduce RP2Track, a new framework that integrates Relative Position Perception through two complementary modules: Rotary Attention Module (RAM) and Spatial Grouping Attention Module (SGAM). RAM employs Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) to encode relative positional information via rotational transformations, enabling finer-grained capture of local spatial relationships essential for maintaining accurate object trajectories. SGAM enhances target state updates by implementing a spatial grouping attention mechanism, which dynamicaly clusters objects based on proximity and computes local attention within groups. This approach improves the precision of state updates by focusing on intra-group interactions. Extensive evaluations across multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the efficacy of RP2Track. Our framework achieves a HOTA score of 75.5 on the DanceTrack dataset and 79.4 on the SportsMOT dataset, establishing new state-of-the-art benchmarks. Additionally, RP2Track exhibits competitive performance on the MOT17 dataset, outperforming existing Transformer-based models. These results validate the effectiveness and superiority of our approach, highlighting its potential to advance the field of MOT. Published: 2026-07-20T20:22:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ziyi Liu; Canbin Zhang; Xueli Li; Bin Fan; Yi Wu; Hongmin Liu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3715054

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

Transformer-based methods have achieved remarkable success in Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) by leveraging attention mechanisms to capture global dependencies. However, these approaches typically inherit design frameworks from object detection tasks, failing to address the unique challenges of MOT—particularly the critical role of relative positional information. This limitation hinders their performance in complex scenarios involving interactive and dynamic targets. To address this, we introduce RP2Track, a new framework that integrates Relative Position Perception through two complementary modules: Rotary Attention Module (RAM) and Spatial Grouping Attention Module (SGAM). RAM employs Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) to encode relative positional information via rotational transformations, enabling finer-grained capture of local spatial relationships essential for maintaining accurate object trajectories. SGAM enhances target state updates by implementing a spatial grouping attention mechanism, which dynamicaly clusters objects based on proximity and computes local attention within groups. This approach improves the precision of state updates by focusing on intra-group interactions. Extensive evaluations across multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the efficacy of RP2Track. Our framework achieves a HOTA score of 75.5 on the DanceTrack dataset and 79.4 on the SportsMOT dataset, establishing new state-of-the-art benchmarks. Additionally, RP2Track exhibits competitive performance on the MOT17 dataset, outperforming existing Transformer-based models. These results validate the effectiveness and superiority of our approach, highlighting its potential to advance the field of MOT.

item.title: MRM++: Enhanced Masked Relation Modeling for Multi-Modal Medical Pre-training
item.dc:creator: Qiushi Yang
Wuyang Li
Zhe Peng
Fangxiao Cheng
Yixuan Yuan
item.description: Abstract Recent progress in deep learning for automated multi-modal medical diagnosis heavily depend on extensive expert annotations, which is time-intensive and impractical. To mitigate this, masked image modeling (MIM)-based pre-training strategies have emerged, effectively learning generalized representations from unlabelled data for various downstream tasks. Nevertheless, these approaches are tailored for natural images while neglect the distinct characteristics of medical data, resulting in suboptimal generalization in medical diagnosis applications. In this work, we attempt to harness the complementary information of multi-modal medical data to perform self-supervised pre-training and propose MRM++, an enhanced masked relation modeling paradigm. Different from the previous MIM methods that randomly mask input data, causing potentially missing of disease-relevant semantics, we devise prior-guided relation masking to break token-wise feature relation guided by anatomy-aware prior in both self- and cross-modal aspects. This can preserve complete input semantics and enable the model to learn abundant disease-related knowledge. Furthermore, to boost semantic relation modeling, the relation matching is introduced, which aligns sample-wise relations among unmasked and masked features. By exploiting inter-sample relations, the relation matching imposes the global constraints in the feature space, ensuring ample semantic relation for robust feature representation. Additionally, considering that the model may overfit to the pre-training dataset and lead to inherent gap between pre-training and downstream fine-tuning, we conceive task-oriented adapting as a pre-stage before fine-tuning to simultaneously perform self-supervised and task-supervised learning on downstream dataset. It can adaptively transform knowledge from the pre-trained model to be compatible with downstream tasks while maintaining transferable information. Extensive experiments on medical image-text and image-genome benchmarks validate the effectiveness and transfer ability of the proposed framework, outperforming state-of-the-art methods across various downstream diagnostic tasks. Source codes are made publicly available on https://github.com/CUHK-AIM-Group/MRM_plus . Published: 2026-07-21T05:32:10+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qiushi Yang; Wuyang Li; Zhe Peng; Fangxiao Cheng; Yixuan Yuan

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02938-5

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

Abstract Recent progress in deep learning for automated multi-modal medical diagnosis heavily depend on extensive expert annotations, which is time-intensive and impractical. To mitigate this, masked image modeling (MIM)-based pre-training strategies have emerged, effectively learning generalized representations from unlabelled data for various downstream tasks. Nevertheless, these approaches are tailored for natural images while neglect the distinct characteristics of medical data, resulting in suboptimal generalization in medical diagnosis applications. In this work, we attempt to harness the complementary information of multi-modal medical data to perform self-supervised pre-training and propose MRM++, an enhanced masked relation modeling paradigm. Different from the previous MIM methods that randomly mask input data, causing potentially missing of disease-relevant semantics, we devise prior-guided relation masking to break token-wise feature relation guided by anatomy-aware prior in both self- and cross-modal aspects. This can preserve complete input semantics and enable the model to learn abundant disease-related knowledge. Furthermore, to boost semantic relation modeling, the relation matching is introduced, which aligns sample-wise relations among unmasked and masked features. By exploiting inter-sample relations, the relation matching imposes the global constraints in the feature space, ensuring ample semantic relation for robust feature representation. Additionally, considering that the model may overfit to the pre-training dataset and lead to inherent gap between pre-training and downstream fine-tuning, we conceive task-oriented adapting as a pre-stage before fine-tuning to simultaneously perform self-supervised and task-supervised learning on downstream dataset. It can adaptively transform knowledge from the pre-trained model to be compatible with downstream tasks while maintaining transferable information. Extensive experiments on medical image-text and image-genome benchmarks validate the effectiveness and transfer ability of the proposed framework, outperforming state-of-the-art methods across various downstream diagnostic tasks. Source codes are made publicly available on https://github.com/CUHK-AIM-Group/MRM_plus .

item.title: Adaptive Quantization for Stable Knowledge Acquisition in Quantization-Aware Continual Learning
item.dc:creator: De Cheng
Kun Gu
Lingfeng He
Huaijie Wang
Di Xu
Nannan Wang
Xinbo Gao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: With the growing demand for efficient and adaptive intelligence on edge devices, deploying deep models under limited memory and computation constraints has become increasingly important. However, existing quantization-aware training (QAT) methods, primarily designed for single-task learning, struggle in dynamic environments where models must learn from a continuous stream of tasks. This often leads to severe catastrophic forgetting and significant representation drift. While continual learning (CL) offers mechanisms for preserving prior knowledge, it typically incurs substantial computational overhead. To address these challenges, we propose Adaptive Quantization for Stable Knowledge Acquisition (AQCL), a unified framework that integrates QAT and CL to achieve both hardware efficiency and lifelong adaptability. AQCL preserves essential knowledge while maintaining model plasticity under low-bit quantization. The framework incorporates two key components: Representation-Preserving Quantization (RPQ), which combines sensitivity-aware bit allocation with hierarchical feature fusion to preserve layers that encode critical information; and Similarity-Aware Orthogonal Update (SAOU), which mitigates feature drift by retaining gradient components orthogonal to prior task subspaces while adaptively scaling task-relevant directions to support learning of new information. Together, these modules ensure stable knowledge retention and effective task adaptation, even under aggressive quantization. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and bit-width settings demonstrate that AQCL consistently outperforms existing approaches, underscoring its promise for efficient, on-device lifelong learning. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/AQCL-472B/. Published: 2026-07-20T20:22:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.783 (must_read)

Authors: De Cheng; Kun Gu; Lingfeng He; Huaijie Wang; Di Xu; Nannan Wang; Xinbo Gao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3715272

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

With the growing demand for efficient and adaptive intelligence on edge devices, deploying deep models under limited memory and computation constraints has become increasingly important. However, existing quantization-aware training (QAT) methods, primarily designed for single-task learning, struggle in dynamic environments where models must learn from a continuous stream of tasks. This often leads to severe catastrophic forgetting and significant representation drift. While continual learning (CL) offers mechanisms for preserving prior knowledge, it typically incurs substantial computational overhead. To address these challenges, we propose Adaptive Quantization for Stable Knowledge Acquisition (AQCL), a unified framework that integrates QAT and CL to achieve both hardware efficiency and lifelong adaptability. AQCL preserves essential knowledge while maintaining model plasticity under low-bit quantization. The framework incorporates two key components: Representation-Preserving Quantization (RPQ), which combines sensitivity-aware bit allocation with hierarchical feature fusion to preserve layers that encode critical information; and Similarity-Aware Orthogonal Update (SAOU), which mitigates feature drift by retaining gradient components orthogonal to prior task subspaces while adaptively scaling task-relevant directions to support learning of new information. Together, these modules ensure stable knowledge retention and effective task adaptation, even under aggressive quantization. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and bit-width settings demonstrate that AQCL consistently outperforms existing approaches, underscoring its promise for efficient, on-device lifelong learning. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/AQCL-472B/.

item.title: Spatiotemporal Predictive Pre-training for Robotic Motor Control
item.dc:creator: Jiange Yang
Bei Liu
Jianlong Fu
Bocheng Pan
Gangshan Wu
Limin Wang
item.description: Robotic motor control necessitates the ability to predict the dynamics of interactions with environment and objects. However, existing self-supervised pre-trained visual representations in robotic motor control, leveraging large-scale egocentric videos, often focus solely on learning the static content features. Thus it neglects the crucial motion clues in human videos, which contain key knowledge about interaction with the environments and objects. In this paper, we present a simple yet effective visual pre-training framework termed as STP for robotic motor control, which jointly performs spatial and temporal prediction with different masking ratios and a dual-decoder design by utilizing large-scale egocentric video data. Specifically, given paired frames in a video clip, we first employ a spatial decoder to perform spatial prediction on the masked current frame to learn the content features. Second, we employ a separate temporal decoder to conduct temporal prediction, conditioned on the masked current frame and an extremely highly-masked future frame, for capturing motion features. This asymmetric masking and decoupled decoding design differentiate our STP from the traditional image or video masked autoencoders by explicitly ensuring the learned image representation to focus more on motion information while still capture spatial details. Due to its lightweight temporal decoder and highly masked future frame, STP incurs manageable extra training overhead over standard image MAE. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization abilities of STP, especially in generalizing to unseen environments with more distractors. Additionally, we extend this framework with post-training and hybrid pre-training to demonstrate the generality and data efficiency of STP. The source code and models will be available at https://github.com/MCG-NJU/STP. Published: 2026-07-20T15:00:39+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.781 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiange Yang; Bei Liu; Jianlong Fu; Bocheng Pan; Gangshan Wu; Limin Wang

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02948-3

Relevance: 0.781 (consider)

Robotic motor control necessitates the ability to predict the dynamics of interactions with environment and objects. However, existing self-supervised pre-trained visual representations in robotic motor control, leveraging large-scale egocentric videos, often focus solely on learning the static content features. Thus it neglects the crucial motion clues in human videos, which contain key knowledge about interaction with the environments and objects. In this paper, we present a simple yet effective visual pre-training framework termed as STP for robotic motor control, which jointly performs spatial and temporal prediction with different masking ratios and a dual-decoder design by utilizing large-scale egocentric video data. Specifically, given paired frames in a video clip, we first employ a spatial decoder to perform spatial prediction on the masked current frame to learn the content features. Second, we employ a separate temporal decoder to conduct temporal prediction, conditioned on the masked current frame and an extremely highly-masked future frame, for capturing motion features. This asymmetric masking and decoupled decoding design differentiate our STP from the traditional image or video masked autoencoders by explicitly ensuring the learned image representation to focus more on motion information while still capture spatial details. Due to its lightweight temporal decoder and highly masked future frame, STP incurs manageable extra training overhead over standard image MAE. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization abilities of STP, especially in generalizing to unseen environments with more distractors. Additionally, we extend this framework with post-training and hybrid pre-training to demonstrate the generality and data efficiency of STP. The source code and models will be available at https://github.com/MCG-NJU/STP.

item.title: MosyMamba: Modal-Synergy Mamba Network for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
item.dc:creator: Mingjin Zhang
Zhongkai Yang
Jie Guo
Yunsong Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion aims to overcome the hardware limitations of the imaging system by fusing low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) with high-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI) to generate high-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI). Existing vision Mamba models mainly perform spatial sequence modeling, which insufficiently exploits the rich spectral information of LR-HSI and limits effective interaction with the spatial details of HR-MSI. To address this, we propose a novel modal-synergy Mamba network for Hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion (Mosy-Mamba) in this paper. Specifically, we design an efficient crossmodality interaction mamba block (CIMB), wherein a synergistic scanning mechanism facilitates detailed interaction between the spectral information of LR-HSI and the spatial geometry of HR-MSI. This approach enables comprehensive contextual extraction and pixel-level spatial-spectral understanding. Furthermore, we introduce a difference-perceptive fusion module (DPFM) with an adaptive feature difference-aware fusion strategy to maintain spatial-spectral consistency. The module perceives the unique information required by spatial and spectral features and dynamically supplies crucial details for them, thereby enhancing the model performance effectively. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method over state-of-the-art approaches across three widely used datasets, e.g., achieving a 1.9027dB gain in PSNR and a 20.58% improvement in SAM on the CAVE dataset. Published: 2026-07-20T20:19:54+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.780 (consider)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3715225

Relevance: 0.780 (consider)

Hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion aims to overcome the hardware limitations of the imaging system by fusing low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) with high-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI) to generate high-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI). Existing vision Mamba models mainly perform spatial sequence modeling, which insufficiently exploits the rich spectral information of LR-HSI and limits effective interaction with the spatial details of HR-MSI. To address this, we propose a novel modal-synergy Mamba network for Hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion (Mosy-Mamba) in this paper. Specifically, we design an efficient crossmodality interaction mamba block (CIMB), wherein a synergistic scanning mechanism facilitates detailed interaction between the spectral information of LR-HSI and the spatial geometry of HR-MSI. This approach enables comprehensive contextual extraction and pixel-level spatial-spectral understanding. Furthermore, we introduce a difference-perceptive fusion module (DPFM) with an adaptive feature difference-aware fusion strategy to maintain spatial-spectral consistency. The module perceives the unique information required by spatial and spectral features and dynamically supplies crucial details for them, thereby enhancing the model performance effectively. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method over state-of-the-art approaches across three widely used datasets, e.g., achieving a 1.9027dB gain in PSNR and a 20.58% improvement in SAM on the CAVE dataset.

item.title: 平行图像：从理论框架到多模态智能视觉应用
item.dc:creator: Zhang Hui
Tian Yonglin
Wang Yutong
Gou Chao
Li Xuan
Wang Feiyue
item.description: 在深度学习推动下，视觉感知系统在智能驾驶、安防监控和医疗诊断等领域取得显著进展。然而，现实场景中数据分布的极度不均衡、长尾样本的稀缺性及高昂的人工标注成本，已成为制约模型性能与泛化能力的关键瓶颈。平行图像作为基于平行系统理论发展而来的新型图像生成与建模方法体系，通过构建人工场景系统、开展计算实验推演及虚实平行执行，形成“建模—训练—反馈—优化”的闭环机制，为视觉感知系统提供了高质量、多样化、结构化的合成数据支撑。本文系统梳理了平行图像的理论基础与发展脉络，重点综述其在虚拟场景生成、多模态特征融合、虚实域迁移和异构知识驱动的平行推理等关键技术路径上的研究进展与应用探索。同时，结合当前生成式人工智能和大规模多模态基础模型的发展趋势，分析了平行图像在智能视觉系统演化中的融合潜力。最后，本文指出该领域面临的主要挑战，并对未来的研究方向和应用前景进行了展望。 Published: 2026-07-21T00:55:25+00:00 Venue: Journal of Image and Graphics Score: 0.779 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhang Hui; Tian Yonglin; Wang Yutong; Gou Chao; Li Xuan; Wang Feiyue

Journal: Journal of Image and Graphics

DOI: 10.11834/jig.250231

Relevance: 0.779 (consider)

在深度学习推动下，视觉感知系统在智能驾驶、安防监控和医疗诊断等领域取得显著进展。然而，现实场景中数据分布的极度不均衡、长尾样本的稀缺性及高昂的人工标注成本，已成为制约模型性能与泛化能力的关键瓶颈。平行图像作为基于平行系统理论发展而来的新型图像生成与建模方法体系，通过构建人工场景系统、开展计算实验推演及虚实平行执行，形成“建模—训练—反馈—优化”的闭环机制，为视觉感知系统提供了高质量、多样化、结构化的合成数据支撑。本文系统梳理了平行图像的理论基础与发展脉络，重点综述其在虚拟场景生成、多模态特征融合、虚实域迁移和异构知识驱动的平行推理等关键技术路径上的研究进展与应用探索。同时，结合当前生成式人工智能和大规模多模态基础模型的发展趋势，分析了平行图像在智能视觉系统演化中的融合潜力。最后，本文指出该领域面临的主要挑战，并对未来的研究方向和应用前景进行了展望。
